I prefer to have those tests (a) in individual test case methods and (b) using assertRaisesRegex to at least roughly check you are catching the error you are expecting to catch.

* (a) brings the advantage of overview if a single condition is violated and sense of graveness if the whole method is borked
* (b) helps with testing that it’s not a "raise RuntimeError()" at the beginning but actual different code paths being taken

The katcp message class does a different serialisation depending on whether
you do repr or str -- the repr one 'pretty-prints' it, which has the
disadvantage that spaces are unescaped, which means you can no longer
descern from the logs whether you have multiple arguments or a single
argument containing spaces.

In CouchDB `_db_updates` works a bit differently from `Cloudant`.  There is no "since" option and a `longpoll` feed always starts from the point from which the request is made to the server.  As such, there is no point to prime the server with any new databases because they would be irrelevant to the `longpoll` request.  Also, the `longpoll` behavior appears to be a "one and done" type situation.  Meaning that a `longpoll` feed is waiting for a single "event" to occur after which it terminates and a heartbeat constitutes an event, so there is no opportunity to create a database as part of the feed loop either since the first event will always be a heartbeat in this test.

I think `ValidationError` is the semantically correct one here.

- `MalformedMessage`: The data payload sent is not well formed and thus cannot be decoded/parsed/etc.
- `ValidationError`: The data is well formed, but the actual content of it does not pass some validation check (like that they sent us the stuff we asked for).

Also, this exception doesn't escape the Manager's internal message loop and is used a signal from `Request.validate_response` to indicate that the response does not validate and thus should not fulfil the request.

I think a nicer and more general solution is to use `io.BytesIO`, open the stream with `open` and use the `t` mode to open the stream in text mode. Then, one can just use `json.dump` rather than `json.dumps`.

The thing is that `json.dumps` will serialize the given object to a string, which creates problems when trying to write Python 2 & 3 compatible code.

My proposed solution would be:
```python
json_file = io.BytesIO()
with open(json_file, mode='wt') as f:
    json.dump({'foo': 'bar'}, f)
# set seek position of the binary stream object back to 0
json_file.seek(0)

isfile_mock = MagicMock(return_value=True)
open_mock = MagicMock(return_value=json_file)
with patch.object(os.path, 'isfile', isfile_mock):
    with patch.object(builtins, 'open', open_mock):
        data.save()
```

I used a similar thing in [`test_test_framework`'s tests](https://github.com/genialis/resolwe/blob/master/resolwe/flow/tests/test_test_framework.py#L64-L89).

Note that I would also drop `_mock` from the `json_file_mock` variable name since the variable is not a Mock object but rather a real in-memory file.

cursor = self.picked

Since the section is about "updating", you may wish to do `pip install --upgrade -r requirements.txt` (or `-U`).

If you don't specify `--upgrade`, pip will say `Requirements already satisfied` for those dependencies which are currently installed from their git repositories but which do not regularly modify their package version.

E.g. MutatorMath tells pip that its package version is 1.8, but that hasn't changed in years despite many changes to the code. So pip does not update because it must assume the requirement is already satisfied (i.e. you already have a MutatorMath==1.8 installed)

For fonttools, since the version string is computed from git metadata, pip is still able to detect that the package version is different, if the commit hash in the requirements.txt changes.

If I understand correctly, `from builtins import str` is a no-op in py3 but renames py2's unicode type to `str` in py2?  I think this definitely will have consequences. A low-level IO method should always operate on raw bytes (`str` in py2, `bytes` in py3), never on encoded strings.  

However this file only explicitly uses `str` in one line, and that's hacky:

`self.do_write(str(s))`

If `s` is already binary data, no problem, but if it's text, then in python2 that `str(s)` would attempt to encode it using the default encoding, which is not guaranteed to work. 

So really write() should check if the argument is bytes, and throw an exception of not. That way we'll find out eagerly if any call sites do the wrong thing.
